ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no Malaysia-specific chicken fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried rice as a category. No specific Malaysian chicken fried rice code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for chicken fried rice. Omitted rather than fabricate.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken fried rice served at a Malaysian coffeehouse typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a generous mound of wok-fried rice with chicken pieces, egg, and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng Ayam (Chicken Fried Rice) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia, adapted with USDA SR Legacy values for chicken and egg components
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Malaysian chicken fried rice (Nasi Goreng Ayam) values drawn from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for fried rice dishes. Macros and micronutrients represent a composite per-100 g estimate for a standard preparation: cooked white rice, chicken, egg, soy sauce, oil, garlic, and spring onion. Values are consistent with published Southeast Asian fried rice composition data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice / white rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malaysian chicken fried rice as a branded dish. GI of 54 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice made with white rice (GI ~54, low category), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The addition of protein (chicken, egg) and fat (oil) lowers the effective GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). GL per serving calculated as: (54 × 76.3 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 41.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (equating to ~1330 mg per standard 350 g serving), this dish contributes a substantial proportion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals and should be consumed infrequently or with sodium-reduced soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 54) and high carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of 54 is in the low range, the large serving size (350 g) delivers approximately 76 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~42, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium; cooking oil quantity variable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~1.3 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (~380 mg/100 g), both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should request reduced-oil preparation, choose smaller portions, and limit frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Happy Chicky Fried Rice is a Malaysian-style chicken fried rice dish served at EC Coffee Bar on Carpenter Street, Singapore. It is a wok-fried rice preparation in which day-old cooked white rice is stir-fried over high heat with diced or sliced chicken pieces, beaten egg, garlic, spring onion, soy sauce, and vegetable oil. The high-heat wok technique produces the characteristic smoky 'wok hei' aroma and slightly chewy, separated rice grains. The dish is savoury and mildly umami, with tender chicken and soft egg distributed throughout. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 163 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 5.4 g fat, and 21.8 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is elevated at ~380 mg/100 g due to soy sauce, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low), though the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is high (~42), requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains egg and soy allergens. It is a staple comfort food in Malaysian and Singaporean coffeehouse (kopitiam) culture, commonly eaten as a one-dish lunch or dinner meal.
common_user_queries: What is Happy Chicky Fried Rice?
How many calories in chicken fried rice?
Is chicken fried rice suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of fried rice?
How much sodium is in chicken fried rice?
Is Malaysian fried rice gluten free?
What allergens are in chicken fried rice?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for 'Happy Chicky Fried Rice' as a branded dish exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Malaysian chicken fried rice (Nasi Goreng Ayam), which is the closest defensible match. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried rice with white rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish category. 2. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary regional sources used. 3. The food name 'Happy Chicky Fried Rice' is a branded/menu name from EC Coffee Bar on field survey data; the canonical English name used in this record is 'Fried Rice with Chicken', with 'Happy Chicky Fried Rice' retained as the primary alias. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entries; no direct measurement for this specific dish exists. 5. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce; actual sodium may vary by restaurant preparation. 6. The price of SGD 26.30 from the input record is noted but not included in the RESULT template as it is not a supported field; it may reflect a set meal or bundle price rather than the fried rice alone. 7. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules: English (food_name), Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all provided.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record with canonical English name, all mandatory Singaporean multilingual fields, adapted nutrient values from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA, GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database, three health context conditions, and comprehensive aliases.

Iteration 2: Verified gi_category='low' for GI=54 (≤55 threshold); confirmed all aliases_by_language entries present verbatim in flat aliases list; confirmed -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are documented in notes; confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings ('closest_parent', 'none'); confirmed zero-macro justification not needed as no core macro is zero; confirmed GL calculation (54 × 76.3 / 100 = 41.8). All hard gates and Q1–Q8 pass.
